title: Pipeline Queue Time
dataSources: CI/CD queue metrics (time in queue, pending jobs)
app: Splunk App for Jenkins, CI/CD system metrics
spl:
index=cicd sourcetype="jenkins:queue"
| timechart span=15m avg(wait_time_sec) as avg_wait, max(queue_length) as max_queue
| where avg_wait > 300
description: Long queue times indicate insufficient CI/CD runner capacity, slowing developer feedback loops and delivery velocity.
value: Long queue times indicate insufficient CI/CD runner capacity, slowing developer feedback loops and delivery velocity.
implementation: Track job queue wait times and queue lengths. Alert when average wait exceeds 5 minutes. Monitor runner/agent utilization. Report on peak hours to guide scaling decisions.
visualization: Line chart (queue time trend), Bar chart (queue time by pipeline), Single value (current queue length).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk App for Jenkins, CI/CD system metrics.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CI/CD queue metrics (time in queue, pending jobs).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track job queue wait times and queue lengths. Alert when average wait exceeds 5 minutes. Monitor runner/agent utilization. Report on peak hours to guide scaling decisions.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=cicd sourcetype="jenkins:queue"
| timechart span=15m avg(wait_time_sec) as avg_wait, max(queue_length) as max_queue
| where avg_wait > 300
```

Understanding this SPL

**Pipeline Queue Time** — Long queue times indicate insufficient CI/CD runner capacity, slowing developer feedback loops and delivery velocity.

Documented **Data sources**: CI/CD queue metrics (time in queue, pending jobs). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk App for Jenkins, CI/CD system metrics. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: cicd; **sourcetype**: jenkins:queue. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=cicd, sourcetype="jenkins:queue". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=15m** buckets — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.
• Filters the current rows with `where avg_wait > 300` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with Jenkins build history. Verify field extractions and index permissions.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (queue time trend), Bar chart (queue time by pipeline), Single value (current queue length).
grandmaExplanation: We watch queueing and wait time so developers are not stuck behind broken runners or clogged pipelines.
knownFalsePositives: Build times vary with cache state, runner availability, or test suite expansion.
